title: Greetings & Goodbyes
shortSummary: Learn how to greet people, introduce yourself, and say goodbye in Spanish — the foundation of every conversation.
culturalNote: In Spain and Latin America, greetings are warmer and more physical than in English-speaking countries. It's common to greet with a kiss on the cheek (one in Latin America, two in Spain) even when meeting someone for the first time in social settings. Skipping a greeting is considered rude — always say hello before jumping into business.

keyPhrases.context: Universal greeting, works in any situation
Used after sunset — both as greeting and farewell
Informal — use with friends and peers
Formal — use with elders, strangers, in professional settings
Casual farewell — more common than adiós in daily life
Said when meeting someone for the first time

grammar.explanation: Spanish has two ways to say 'you': tú (informal) and usted (formal). Use tú with friends, family, and people your age. Use usted with strangers, elders, and in professional contexts.
grammar.commonMistake: Using tú with a stranger or elderly person — this can come across as disrespectful in many Spanish-speaking cultures.

grammar.explanation: In Spanish, adjectives change to match the gender of the noun. 'Día' is masculine so it's 'buenos días', but 'tarde' and 'noche' are feminine so it's 'buenas tardes' and 'buenas noches'.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying 'buenas días' or 'buenos noches' — mixing up the gender agreement.

dialogue.setup: María meets her new neighbour Carlos at the apartment building entrance on a sunny morning.

lines.speaker: María
lines.english: Very well, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Bien, gracias. Soy Carlos, su nuevo vecino.
lines.english: Fine, thanks. I'm Carlos, your new neighbour.

lines.speaker: María
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Carlos! I'm María.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Nice to meet you, María. Well, see you later!

lines.speaker: María
lines.english: See you later! Have a good day!

quiz.question: How do you say 'Good afternoon' in Spanish?
Which greeting would you use at 9 PM?
quiz.explanation: 'Buenas noches' is used after sunset, both as a greeting and a farewell.

quiz.question: ¿Cómo _____ usted? (formal 'how are you')
quiz.explanation: With 'usted' (formal you), we use the third person form 'está'.

quiz.question: When meeting someone for the first time, you say:
quiz.explanation: 'Mucho gusto' literally means 'much pleasure' and is the standard way to say 'nice to meet you'.

quiz.question: Arrange: bien / estoy / gracias
quiz.explanation: The verb 'estoy' comes first, followed by the adjective 'bien', then 'gracias'.
